nodes.body: A `Retry-After: 0` response is parsed as a valid header but then falls through to the normal exponential backoff because the caller only treats `retryWait > 0` as handled. Slack can use `0` to mean retry immediately, so this currently waits the backoff interval instead of honoring the header; track whether the header was present separately from the duration value so zero-second retry-after skips the exponential sleep.

This sleeps for `Retry-After` even when the current attempt is the last permitted attempt (`attempt == backoff.RetryCount`). In that case no retry will be made, so a final 429 with `Retry-After` can block `Flush` for up to the cumulative wait cap before returning an error. Check that another attempt remains before sleeping on `Retry-After`.

This test does not exercise the Slack notification path: it logs to `debugLogger := slog.New(spy)`, so the counted `Handle` call is the debug logger's own handler, not evidence that a `SlackHandler` is isolated from DebugLogger writes. As written the test would still pass if a real Slack handler were accidentally wired as the debug logger output; use an actual `SlackHandler`/mock server or remove the misleading coverage.

This reflection only inspects fields on `SlackHandler`; it cannot detect an exported package-level `*slog.Logger`, which is what the test name and comment say it is guarding against. This gives a false sense of coverage for the notification-path isolation requirement; either narrow the test to `SlackHandler` fields or replace it with a check that can actually cover exported package symbols.

This checked item still says Phase 2 rebuilds a logger/fan-out that includes Slack handlers, but `cmd/tlsrpt-digest/main.go` now intentionally keeps Slack handlers separate from `slog.Default()` and returns `[]*SlackHandler` for explicit typed-helper/Flush use. The plan should be updated so it no longer documents the previously removed logger fan-out design.

The success criteria still describe rebuilding a bundled logger after Phase 2, but the implementation no longer wires Slack handlers into `slog.Default()`; `setupNotifyHandlers` returns handlers for the processing loop to flush explicitly. This makes the implementation plan inconsistent with the current bootstrap design.

This test constant is likely to trip the repository's security lint: `make lint` runs `.golangci-security.yml`, which enables gosec G101 (hardcoded credentials), and the identifier contains `secret`/`token` with a credential-looking value. Rename the fixture to avoid credential keywords or add a narrowly scoped `//nolint:gosec` justification so the security lint remains green.

The nil-client path does not create an `http.Client` with `Timeout: 5s`; `postWithRetry` uses `&http.Client{}` and enforces the 5-second limit with a per-request context. Please adjust this comment so callers do not rely on the injected/default client's `Timeout` field being set.

This comment is stale: the DryRunNoURL branch below now returns two handlers (one `LevelModeExactInfo` and one `LevelModeWarnAndAbove`), not a single handler. Update the comment so callers understand that dry-run without URLs covers both INFO and WARN/ERROR paths.

`url.Parse` errors include the input URL in their `Error()` string (for example, `parse "...": ...`), so wrapping `err` here can expose the Slack webhook token in startup logs when the URL is malformed. Return a sanitized validation message that does not include the raw parse error or strip the URL before logging/wrapping it.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

The maxCumulativeWait is set to 30 seconds, but the implementation plan (Step 2-2) specifies a total time limit of 34 seconds for 4 attempts (initial + 3 retries). With a 5-second request timeout per attempt, 4 attempts take up to 20 seconds. To maintain the 34-second guarantee, the total wait time should be capped at 14 seconds (34s - 20s).

```suggestion
	maxCumulativeWait = 14 * time.Second
```

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

Replacing the original postErr with a generic errRequestFailed sentinel loses valuable debugging information (e.g., whether it was a DNS failure, connection refused, or a TLS handshake error). While the intent might be to avoid leaking the webhook URL contained in *url.Error, it is better to wrap the original error and redact the URL in the string representation.

```suggestion
		return false, 0, &SlackServerError{StatusCode: 0, Cause: postErr}
```
